skill.name: python-executor
skill.description: Runs Python code through the inference.sh sandbox using the belt CLI, with common data, web, media, document, and automation libraries available.
skill.summary: Execute Python scripts in an inference.sh sandbox with preinstalled data, web, media, and document libraries.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: Static shell-execution alerts are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline code in SKILL.md. The real risk is intentional execution of user-provided Python through the external belt CLI, with network-capable libraries and returned output files. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: User-Provided Python Execution Through External CLI
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for elevated execution risk. The skill allows Bash access to belt and shows belt app run accepting a code payload, so prompts can cause arbitrary Python to execute in the external sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry and quick-start command directly show the execution path. The file describes sandboxing, so this is elevated legitimate functionality rather than confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Execution Examples
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for network capability. The skill lists HTTP and browser automation libraries and includes examples that request external sites, which can be misused if sensitive data is placed in executed code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network libraries and outbound request examples are explicit. I did not find evidence that the skill itself exfiltrates credentials without user-supplied code.

medium_findings.title: Executed Code Can Return Generated Files
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for data-handling risk. Files saved under outputs are automatically returned, so generated files could include sensitive data if user prompts or code place secrets there.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-output behavior is documented and specific. The risk depends on user-provided code and data, so the concern is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The many Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown inline code, tables, and fenced examples rather than Ruby backtick execution in runnable source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is documentation, and the backticks delimit Markdown code spans or code blocks. No Ruby file or Ruby execution context exists in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Alerts Lack Malicious Evidence
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-cryptography alert points to the skill description URL, and the network-reconnaissance alert points to a note about isolated subprocess execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic code, scanning commands, or reconnaissance logic. They are descriptive Markdown text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Example URLs
low_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW as a minor publishing concern. The hardcoded URLs are image, install, documentation, and example API links, not hidden endpoints, but users should understand that examples may contact third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation and examples. There is no evidence of concealed exfiltration, but network behavior is part of the skill capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Allowlist Grants belt Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed tool declaration permits Bash calls matching belt, enabling the skill workflow to invoke an external execution service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools line is explicit and directly supports the execution workflow. It is not malicious by itself, but it is a sensitive capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Libraries Available to Executed Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented environment includes HTTP clients and browser automation libraries that can make outbound requests from executed Python code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The library list directly names network-capable packages. Actual network use depends on user code submitted to the sandbox.

content.value_statement: Agents often need Python execution without setting up a local environment. This skill runs scripts through inference.sh with common libraries for data, web, media, and document tasks.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python 3.10 code through the inference.sh app runner.
Supports data processing with NumPy, Pandas, SciPy, and visualization libraries.
Can perform HTTP requests, HTML parsing, and browser automation from submitted Python code.
Creates image, video, audio, PDF, SVG, and 3D output files in the sandbox.
Returns files saved in the outputs directory to the calling workflow.
Offers default and high-memory variants for larger jobs.
limitations: Requires an installed and authenticated inference.sh belt CLI.
Runs on CPU only and does not provide GPU or dedicated machine learning runtimes.
Execution is non-interactive, so scripts must save outputs instead of opening windows.
Timeouts are limited to the documented range of 1 to 300 seconds.

use_cases.title: Analyze a small business dataset
use_cases.description: Load a CSV, clean fields, calculate totals, and return a chart or summary file.

use_cases.title: Collect public web data
use_cases.description: Fetch a public page or API, parse the response, and return structured results.

use_cases.title: Generate media and document assets
use_cases.description: Create charts, images, short videos, PDFs, SVG files, or 3D exports from script instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Run a simple calculation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation of the Python executor.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to run a short Python script that calculates summary statistics for these numbers: [12, 18, 21, 27, 30]. Return the printed result.

prompt_templates.title: Create a chart from tabular data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to create a bar chart from this table: product A 42, product B 57, product C 31. Save the chart as an output file and summarize the result.

prompt_templates.title: Scrape and summarize a public page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to fetch the public page I provide, extract the page title and main headings, and return a short summary with no private data included.

prompt_templates.title: Build a multi-file analysis artifact
prompt_templates.prompt: Use python-executor to clean this dataset, generate a chart, export a CSV of normalized rows, and create a short PDF report explaining the findings.

output_examples.input: Analyze a small sales table and create a chart.
output: Printed summary of total and average sales.
Returned chart image showing sales by category.

output_examples.input: Fetch a public API profile and summarize selected fields.
output_examples.output: A concise text summary of the returned public data, with no credentials or private tokens included.
A returned 3D model file plus a short message describing vertex count and export status.

best_practices: Review Python code before execution, especially when it contains network requests or file output.
Keep inputs small enough for the timeout and memory limits, then use the high-memory variant only when needed.
Avoid placing secrets, tokens, private records, or credentials in prompts or generated output files.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for untrusted code that has not been reviewed.
Do not rely on interactive windows, prompts, or display calls during execution.
Do not scrape websites or APIs without checking permission, rate limits, and data handling requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill run?
faq.answer: It runs Python 3.10 code through the inference.sh python-executor app using the belt CLI.

faq.question: Does it need local Python packages installed?
faq.answer: No. The documented packages are available in the remote sandbox, but the belt CLI must be installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it access the internet?
faq.answer: The documentation includes HTTP libraries and request examples, so submitted code can make network requests.

faq.question: How are generated files returned?
faq.answer: Files saved in the outputs directory are automatically detected and returned by the execution workflow.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill runs code through an external service and can return files or make network requests.

faq.question: Does it support GPU workloads?
faq.answer: No. The documented environment is CPU-only and points users to dedicated AI apps for machine learning image or video tasks.
